Items.Title: AWS CDK 和 DynamoDB：这一配置线要花费您数百美元 | AWS CDK and DynamoDB: This One Configuration Line Is Costing You Hundreds of Dollars
Items.Description:
嘿，无服务器爱好者和爱好者 😶‍🌫️！ 你有没有犯过一个不幸的错误🙊，让你的团队损失了数百美元？ 比那更多的？ 没门。 几千！？ 首先，很抱歉你经历了这些。 其次，我知道你的感受。

使用AWS Cloud Development Kit预置 DynamoDB 表时，CDK 默认将计费模式设置为“预置”而不是按请求付费。 因此，Dynamodb 基本上不再是真正的无服务器——还记得缩放到零吗？

快速修复：实例化您的 AWS Dynamodb 表时，请确保将计费模式明确设置为“按请求付费”。 如果您正在玩玩具堆，您还可以将移除策略设置为“销毁”。 这意味着如果您删除堆栈，表将因此被删除（在生产中使用此设置可能是个坏主意）。

 // code/dynamodb-cdk.ts import { App , RemovalPolicy , Stack } from ' @aws-cdk/core ' ; import { AttributeType , Table , BillingMode } from ' @aws-cdk/aws-dynamodb ' ; const app = new App (); const stack = new Stack ( app ); const table = new Table ( stack , ' MyTable ' , { partitionKey : { name : ' PK ' , type : AttributeType . STRING }, sortKey : { name : ' SK ' , type : AttributeType . STRING }, billingMode : BillingMode . PAY_PER_REQUEST , removalPolicy : RemovalPolicy . DESTROY , });

作为软件工程师熟悉无服务器

作为初级开发人员，我的第一个任务是帮助设计“无服务器 101 课程”。 我们旨在帮助我团队中的未来新手快速熟悉云原生概念。 我们创建了一个简洁的教程，介绍了无服务器的基础知识：lambdas、API 网关、IAM 角色和 dynamodb 表 - AWS 作为我们的云提供商。 我对结果很满意！ 新手终于可以在沙盒环境中玩耍，其中包含复杂的（一开始很吓人）概念，例如功能即服务 (FaaS) 和云原生托管数据库！

我感到很自豪。 我们有一个小协议，每个新开发人员都将部署一个玩具堆栈。 它很好地发挥了作用，无服务器新手喜欢它。

我曾经认为无服务器技术的归零属性是理所当然的 🥺

当然，如果您阅读了 TL;DR 和小背景故事，您就会开始看到我在整个咆哮中的去向。

我们团队的每个新工程师都部署了一个玩具堆栈来了解无服务器和 AWS。 无服务器教程使用 AWS CDK。 为了保持轻松，我们主要使用默认设置。 在这些设置中隐藏了“预配置”计费模式和“保留”删除策略。

为简化起见，通过 DynamoDB“预置”计费模式，AWS 预置了一个地板存储空间，以及最小读取容量单位 (RCU) 和写入容量单位 (WCU)。 然后，无论您使用多少，AWS 都会向您收费。

对于 DynamoDB，我发现即使您根本不使用该表，“供应”计费模式的每个表（在美国地区）的费用约为每月 11 美元。 此外，当删除堆栈时，“保留”删除策略会阻止 DynamoDB 表随堆栈一起删除。

每个菜鸟部署一个沙盒堆栈。 存储资源是预配置的，即即使我们不使用该表，它也会花费一个底数。 防止表被删除。 是的，一个潜在的无底洞被创造出来了。

总结一下：每月检查您的无服务器费用，不要认为无服务器的低价是理所当然的。

与非无服务器技术堆栈相比，无服务器技术成本低廉。 便宜一个数量级。 当作为我们的无服务器教程的一部分部署的堆栈每月花费我们数百美元时，我的经理似乎有点惊讶！

值得庆幸的是 ✨，我的队友在几个月后发现了这个错误，从而限制了我的 oopsie 浪费数千美元（和大量电力💚）。



原文:

Hey there, serverless aficionadas and aficionados 😶‍🌫️! Have you ever made an unfortunate mistake 🙊 that cost your team hundreds of dollars? More than that? No way. Thousands!? First, I'm sorry you went through this. Second, I know how you feel.

TL;DR

When using the AWS Cloud Development Kit to provision a DynamoDB table, CDK sets by default the billing mode as "Provisioned" instead of pay-per-request. Therefore, Dynamodb essentially ceases to be truly serverless - remember scale-to-zero?

Quick fix: when instantiating your AWS Dynamodb table, make sure to explicitly set the billing mode to "pay-per-request". If you're playing around with a toy stack, you can also set the removal policy to "destroy". This means that if you remove your stack, the table will be deleted as a result (probably a bad idea to use this setting in production).

// code/dynamodb-cdk.ts import { App, RemovalPolicy, Stack } from '@aws-cdk/core';
import { AttributeType, Table, BillingMode } from '@aws-cdk/aws-dynamodb'; const app = new App();
const stack = new Stack(app); const table = new Table(stack, 'MyTable', { partitionKey: { name: 'PK', type: AttributeType.STRING }, sortKey: { name: 'SK', type: AttributeType.STRING }, billingMode: BillingMode.PAY_PER_REQUEST, removalPolicy: RemovalPolicy.DESTROY,
});

Getting acquainted with serverless as a software engineer

One of my first missions as a junior dev was to help design a "serverless 101 course". We aimed to help the future rookies in my team feel familiar with cloud-native concepts quickly. We created a concise tutorial that goes through the basics of serverless: lambdas, API gateway, IAM roles and dynamodb tables - with AWS as our cloud provider. I was pretty happy about the result! Newcomers could finally play in a sandbox environment with complex - and scary at first - concepts such as functions-as-a-service (FaaS) and cloud-native managed databases!

I felt proud. We had a small protocol where every new developer would deploy a toy stack. It served its purpose well and serverless newbies liked it.

I used to take serverless tech's scale-to-zero property for granted 🥺

Surely, if you read the TL;DR and the small background story, you're starting to see where I'm going with this whole rant.

Each of our team's new engineer deployed a toy stack to learn about serverless and AWS. The serverless tutorial uses AWS CDK. To keep it light, we mostly went with default settings. Amongst these settings hid the "provisioned" billing mode and the "retain" removal policy.

To simplify, with the DynamoDB "provisioned" billing mode, AWS provisions a floor storage space, and minimum Read capacity units (RCU) & Write capacity units (WCU). Then, AWS charges you for it, regardless of your usage.

For DynamoDB, I found that the "provision" billing mode costs about $11/month per table (in US regions) even if you're not using the table at all. Moreover, when a stack is removed, the "retain" removal policy prevents the DynamoDB table from being deleted with the stack.

Each rookie deploys a sandbox stack. Storage resources are provisioned, i.e. the table costs a floor amount even if we don't use it. Tables are prevented from being deleted. Yep, a potentially bottomless pit was created.

Wrapping it up: check your serverless expenses each month and don't take serverless' low prices for granted.

Serverless is inexpensive compared to non-serverless tech stacks. An order of magnitude cheaper. My manager seemed a little surprised when the stacks deployed as part of our serverless tutorial were costing us hundreds each month!

Thankfully ✨, my teammates caught the error after a couple of months, thus limiting my oopsie from wasting thousands of dollars (and lots of electricity 💚).

RxFire was created by David East, who is a Firebase Developer Advocate. Although Firebase Blog introduced RxFire in September, 2018 I thought it would be a good point of reference for beginners starting out with both ReactJs and Firebase.For those coming from an Angular background you might be familiar with RxJS and the Angularfire2 package. I know from my experience you MUST learn RxJS if you are going to use Angular on any size application. It can be one of the hardest parts to learn but there are several fantastic tutorials and sites dedicated to how RxJS works.Some of my favorites

New to React, Please checkout

Example

In the example below it shows a quick preview of how the final React application will work. As you can see there are essentiall 4 steps that will occur.

  • Sign In

  • Add Cat Fact

Setup

You will need NPM which comes bundled with nodejs.Since we wil be the create-react-app cli you must install this first

You will also need to create your own Firebase project and initialize a Firestore database, please create this using strict mode.

Final App Structure

Install dependencies

You will need both rxfire and firebase

npm i rxfire firebase

Create Firebase.js

Firebase.js will be our main Firebase file and will include the tools for our firebase application, including the instantiation of firebase. This file includes rxfire for both the auth and firestore helper functions.Firebase.js

```plain text import 'firebase/auth'; import 'firebase/firestore'; import firebase from 'firebase/app'; import { authState } from 'rxfire/auth'; import { collectionData } from 'rxfire/firestore'; import { filter } from 'rxjs/operators'; const app = firebase.initializeApp({ /* Place your configuration here */ }); const firestore = firebase.firestore(app); //Initialize firestore const auth = firebase.auth(app); // Initialize firebase auth const loggedIn$ = authState(auth).pipe(filter(user => !!user)); // Observable only return when user is logged in. export { app, auth,firestore, collectionData, loggedIn$ };

export default firebase;




> You can skip the Instructions part of the lesson if you are already familiar with ReactJS.

## Simple Initial Component

### Remove App.js and replace

Remove the core of App.js to start as we will be using the pattern of `extends React.Component`. We will now start simple by adding our first React Component Called Instructions.

import React from "react";
import Instructions from "./components/Instructions";

}

Create AddCat.js

The first button that we are going to add is simple enough it calls an API and pushes the data returned into a firestore collection as a new document. I always like to work from top down, so first lets import AddCat.js into our App.js.

Items.Title: 寻找具有巨大价值的公司 | Finding a Company with Great Values
Items.Description:
有很多公司都有价值观，通常只是被人力资源部安排在那里，因为他们觉得自己“应该”有价值观。 然后还有其他公司围绕他们的价值观建立了他们的整个工作方式。 例如，让我们看看Google 的使命、愿景和价值观。

谷歌使命宣言

组织世界信息并使其普遍可用和有用

谷歌愿景声明

一键获取全球资讯

谷歌价值观

  • 伟大还不够好。

  • 专注于用户，其他一切都会随之而来。

  • 最好把一件事做好。

  • 快比慢好。

  • 网络上的民主有效。

  • 不作恶也能赚钱。

  • 总是有更多的信息。

  • 对信息的需求跨越所有国界。

  • 不穿西装你也可以很严肃。

  • 你不需要在你的办公桌前。

这些都是伟大的目标，我特别认为“你可以不作恶就能赚钱”。 是一个要强调的。 尽管您可能想暂停一下，因为这些目标似乎将公司的成功置于员工之前，但请确保在找工作之前考虑到这一点。

您将在下面找到亚马逊的使命和价值观。 我反复听说亚马逊对客户的关注可能仅次于沃尔特迪斯尼的迎宾员。 重要的是要注意这里有一些好的值。 我特别喜欢“Hire the Best”和“Learn and Be Curious”。

亚马逊使命宣言

亚马逊愿景声明

我们的愿景是成为地球上最以客户为中心的公司； 建立一个人们可以来寻找和发现他们可能想在网上购买的任何东西的地方。

亚马逊价值观

  • 客户至上

  • 所有权

  • 发明和简化

  • 学习和好奇

  • 聘请最好的

  • 最高标准

  • 大想

  • 行动的偏见

  • 赢得信任

  • 交付结果

上面的大型FANG公司只是代表GIANT公司的一个例子。 有很多初创公司、小公司、大学和非营利组织允许更多地关注为公司工作的人以及他们努力维护的价值观。 不要仅仅停留在薪水上，确保你觉得自己在工作中受到赞赏，这将弥补你生命中每一年的 2,080 小时（如果你幸运的话）。

为什么我选择在 OST 工作

许多人不知道我的日常工作是在密歇根州大急流城一家名为OST （开放系统技术）的惊人公司工作。 我决定在 OST 工作的原因是他们的公司文化和价值观。 在 OST，我们向每位员工发放一张蓝卡，上面列出了我们的价值观。 我们下面的口号是我们赖以生存的。 

 We honor our people and their families first, clients second and the rest will fall into place. ~OST



 荣誉——我们的员工和他们的家人。

  • 喜悦——我们的客户。

  • 服务 - 谦虚。

  • 拥抱——创业创新。

  • 学习——通过好奇心和同理心。

    • 找到适合你的东西

    如果你刚刚开始，你很可能会优先考虑你的薪水有多大，医疗保健，401k。 基本上，您的整体薪酬就是您要检查的内容。 然而，当你只把它作为你的第一目标时，它可能会让你在路上痛苦。

    例如，在 2020 年大流行期间，许多人发现他们的工作被解雇了，因为他们的公司只关心达到特定的毛利率、收入或所有者权益数字。 我会警告任何不把员工放在首位的公司。 确保您进行研究并了解公司在过去一年中对这些事件的反应。 不要害怕在你的面试中问这个问题。

    请记住，您是技术领域的宝贵资源。 工作比可用的人多，您应该被视为宝贵的资源！



    原文:

    There are many companies out there that have values, that often are just placed there by Human Resources because they felt they “should” have values. Then there are other companies that build their entire way of working around their values. For instance lets take a look at Google’s Mission, Vision and Values.

    Google Mission Statement

    Organize the world's information and make it universally accessible and useful

    Google Vision Statement

    To provide access to the world’s information in one click

    Google Values

    • Great isn’t good enough.

    • Focus on the user, all else will follow.

    • It’s best to do one thing really well.

    • Fast is better than slow.

    • Democracy on the web works.

    • You can make money without doing evil.

    • There’s always more information.

    • The need for information crosses all borders.

    • You can be serious without a suit.

    • You don’t need to be at your desk.

    These are great goals and I especially think that the "You can make money without doing evil." is one to highlight. Although you might want to take a pause as these goals seem to put the companies success before the employee, just make sure you consider this before taking a job.

    Below you will find Amazon's Mission and Values. I have heard this repeatedly that Amazon's focus on the customer is second probably only to a greeter at Walt Disney. It is important to note that there are some good values in here. I especially like "Hire the Best" and "Learn and Be Curious".

    Amazon Mission Statement

    "Amazon is guided by four principles: customer obsession rather than competitor focus, passion for invention, commitment to operational excellence, and long-term thinking. Customer reviews, 1-Click shopping, personalized recommendations, Prime, Fulfillment by Amazon, AWS, Kindle Direct Publishing, Kindle, Fire tablets, Fire TV, Amazon Echo, and Alexa are some of the products and services pioneered by Amazon."

    Amazon Vision Statement

    Our vision is to be earth's most customer-centric company; to build a place where people can come to find and discover anything they might want to buy online.

    Amazon Values

    • Customer Obsession

    • Ownership

    • Invent and Simplify

    • Learn and Be Curious

    • Hire the Best

    • The Highest Standards

    • Think Big

    • Bias for Action

    • Earn Trust

    • Deliver Results

    The large FANG companies above are only an example that represent GIANT companies. There are a lot of startups, small companies, universities, and non-profits that allow for a much greater focus on those working for the company and the values they are trying to uphold. Don't get stuck on just the paycheck, make sure you feel like you are appreciated at work, it will make up 2,080 hours (if you are lucky) every single year of your life.

    Why I chose to work at OST

    Many don’t know that my day job is at an amazing company called OST (Open Systems Technologies) in Grand Rapids Michigan. The reason that I decided to work at OST was their company culture and values. At OST we have a blue card that is given to every employee that lists our values. Our slogan below is what we live by.

    We honor our people and their families first, clients second and the rest will fall into place. ~OST



    OST’s Five Guiding Principles

    • Honor - our employees and their families.

    • Delight - our clients.

    • Serve - with humility.

    • Embrace - entrepreneurship and innovation.

    • Learn - through curiosity and empathy.

    Find What Works for You

    If you are just getting started you most likely are prioritizing your goals around how big your salary will be, health care, 401k. Basically your overall compensation is what you will be looking to examine. However, when you only focus on this as your number one goal it can cause you pain down the road.

    For instance during the 2020 pandemic many found their job was eliminated because their company only cared about hitting a specific Gross Margin, Revenue, or Owner's Equity number. I would caution against any company that is not putting their employees first. Make sure you do your research and see how a company reacted to the events this past year. Don't be afraid to ask this in your interview as well.

    Remember you are a valuable resource in the technology field. There are more jobs than people available and you should be treated as that valued resource!

Items.Title: 在 React 中构建搜索和过滤功能🦄 | Constructing Search & Filter Functionality in React 🦄
Items.Description:
劳伦泰森🕶

发表于搜索和过滤功能很简单，但我经常需要参考文档。 要记住的最重要的事情是组件之间的信息流——状态在哪里，它在哪里传递，以及通过回调函数将数据从子组件发送到其父组件。 起初，这个功能可能感觉像是一个令人困惑的循环，但在你构建了几次这样的组件之后，基本步骤就变得更容易记住了。

最近我从头开始构建了一个 React 应用程序作为编码 Bootcamp 项目。 该应用程序名为 Scientific Foodie，这是一个共享有关所有不同类型食物（如水果、蔬菜、谷物、葫芦等）的科学信息的数据库：

因为我正在构建一个数据库，所以我决定提供搜索和过滤功能，以便用户可以与数据库进行最充分的交互。

在这个应用程序中，我的搜索和过滤器组件协同工作，所以下面，我将介绍构建每个组件的步骤，以及我如何（这里有一个提示）将它们链接在一起。

由于我的 Search 和 Filter 组件是我的 FoodContainer 组件的兄弟和子组件，我确定 FoodContainer 将保持状态并处理回调函数。 为什么？ 因为兄弟组件不能直接相互传递数据。 数据只能在父母和孩子之间上下流动。 因此，它们的父组件 FoodContainer 将保持状态并处理整体功能。

制作你的过滤器组件📌

一旦您编写了基本的 JSX，即您过滤的内容，就该构建功能了。 过滤器选项的值应与您过滤的数据键相匹配。 我的应用程序按组过滤我的食物。

  1. 在您的父组件中，创建将捕获用户选择的过滤器的状态。 我喜欢将过滤器的默认状态设置为“全部”，这是过滤器组件中的第一个选项。 接下来，我们将创建一个简单的回调函数来处理捕获 event.target.value，即过滤器更改和更新状态。 最初，我总是将我的辅助函数设置为控制台记录选择，这样我就知道我的回调正在工作——稍后会详细介绍。

  2. 将辅助函数作为道具从您的父组件传递给您的子组件，即过滤器组件。 这允许回调由与调用它的组件不同的组件拥有。

  3. 过滤器功能需要一个 onChange 事件。 只需将辅助函数作为道具分配给 onChange。 每次选择过滤器选项时，该值都会通过我们的辅助函数传递给我们的父组件。

  4. 试试这个以确保它有效！ 你怎么知道？ 请记住，我们在父组件的辅助函数中有一个临时控制台日志。 在您的应用程序中，选择一个过滤器选项并查看控制台中记录的值。 同样，确保您的选项值与您的数据匹配。

  5. 既然您知道回调有效，请删除控制台日志并将其替换为您的状态的 setter 函数。 并添加一个临时控制台日志来检查您的状态变量的值。 在您的应用程序中，再次选择一个过滤器，您将看到您的状态在控制台中捕获过滤器的变化！ 最后，删除控制台日志。

制作你的搜索组件🔎

暂时将过滤器组件放在一边，让我们构建我们的搜索。

  1. 在顶级父组件中设置状态，在我的例子中是 FoodContainer。 您的搜索状态的默认值应该是一个空字符串。

  2. 创建一个简单的回调函数来处理在搜索栏中输入的内容。 正如我们最初使用过滤器辅助函数所做的那样，将您的搜索辅助函数设置为控制台记录搜索栏中的文本。 我们要确保它有效。

  3. 接下来，您的 Search 组件应该采用两个 props，即搜索状态变量和辅助函数。 我们将状态作为道具传递，因为我们正在构建一个受控组件。

  4. 在您的 Search 组件中，该值应将搜索状态变量作为道具，而您的 onChange 将辅助回调作为道具。 您的助手正在捕获 event.target.value。

  5. 试试这个以确保它有效！ 在我们的父组件中使用临时控制台登录我们的搜索助手功能，在您的搜索栏中输入一些内容。 在控制台中，您将看到每次击键记录为一个字母。 没关系！ 我们还没有更新状态； 我们只是控制台日志记录以确保回调正常工作。

  6. 接下来，删除该控制台日志并将其替换为搜索状态的 setter 函数。 然后再添加一个临时控制台日志以检查您的状态是否正确更新并在搜索栏中再次输入。 您将在控制台中看到文本，并在搜索栏中看到单词形成。 凉爽的！ 因为这是一个将状态分配为搜索值的受控组件，所以字母会在您键入时出现在搜索栏中，也就是用户正在实时观看状态更新。

  7. 最后，删除最后一个控制台日志。

将过滤器和搜索链接在一起⛓

最后一步是根据搜索和过滤的内容更新页面上显示的项目。 就我而言，我希望我的用户根据食物的通用名称在数据库中搜索特定食物，按食物组过滤，然后一起执行这些操作。

我的父组件 FoodContainer 处理在页面上显示我的食物，所以我添加了以下代码：

  1. 声明保存要映射的数组的变量。 我最初映射了我的整个食物数组，但该数组没有内置我的搜索和过滤逻辑。所以，我创建了一个变量来处理我的搜索和过滤； 这个数组将被映射以显示我的项目。

  2. 写一个 if 语句。 如果选择的过滤器是“All”，那么数组中的所有元素都将返回。 这写为return true 。 否则，返回与用户选择的过滤器匹配的元素。

我们可以在这里停下来； 但是，我们希望搜索和过滤器一起工作，以便用户可以同时使用两者。 还有几个步骤：

  1. 接下来，链接另一个过滤器。 现在我们的变量foodsToDisplay将过滤器和搜索代码都考虑在内，而不是其中之一。 链式方法本质上就是在一个语句中一个接一个地调用方法，让你的代码简洁，逻辑可以继续。

  2. 就我而言，我允许用户按通用名称搜索食物。 添加includes是因为我想在用户键入时返回部分匹配项。 最后，我将通用名称和搜索转换为小写，这样大写就不会妨碍。

现在，最后，让我们映射新数组！ 完成后，尝试在您的应用中进行搜索和过滤。 它太酷了。

那么它对我来说怎么样？ 这是我的 React 项目的结果：

LaurenTyson🕶

Posted on Aug 30

Search and filter functionality is straightforward, yet I often need to refer back to documentation. The biggest thing to remember is the information flow between the components - where does state live, where it's passed, and sending data from a child component to its parent via a callback function. At first, this functionality can feel like a confusing loop, but after you construct components like these a few times, the basic steps get easier to remember.

Recently I built a React app from scratch as a coding Bootcamp project. The app is called Scientific Foodie, a database that shares scientific information about all different types of food, such as fruits, vegetables, grains, gourds, and more:

Because I was building a database, I decided to have search and filter functionality so users could interact with the database to the fullest.

In this app, my Search and Filter components work in tandem, so below, I walk through the steps of constructing each component and how I, and here's a hint, chained them together.

And since my Search and Filter components are siblings and children of my FoodContainer component, I determined that FoodContainer would hold state and handle the callback functions. Why? Because sibling components can't pass data to each other directly. Data can only flow up and down between parent and child. Therefore their parent component, FoodContainer, will hold state and handle the overall functionality.

Making your Filter Component 📌

Once you have the basic JSX written, i.e., what you're filtering by, it's time to construct the functionality. The value of your filter options should match the data key you're filtering. My app filters my food by group.

  1. In your parent component, create state that will capture the user's selected filter. I like to set default state for my filter to "All," which is the first option in my Filter component. "All" ensures everything displays upon page load, and while filtering, the user can return to seeing everything at any point.

  2. Next, we'll create a simple callback function to handle capturing the event.target.value, which is the filter change, and updating state. Initially, I always set my helper function to console log the selection, just so I know my callback is working — more on this in a minute.

  3. Pass the helper function as props from your parent component to your child, the Filter component. This allows the callback to be owned by a different component than the one invoking it.

  4. Filter functionality takes an onChange event. Simply assign the helper function as props to onChange. Each time a filter option is selected, the value is passed up through our helper function to our parent component.

  5. Try this out to make sure it works! How will you know? Remember, we have a temporary console log in the helper function in our parent component. In your app, select a filter option and see the value logged in your console. Again, make sure your option values match your data.

  6. Now that you know the callback works remove the console log and replace it with your state's setter function. And add one more temporary console log to check the value of your state variable. In your app, select a filter again, and you'll see your state capturing the change in filters in the console! Finally, remove the console log.

Making your Search Component 🔎

Leaving the Filter component aside, for now, let's construct our Search.

  1. Set up state in the top-level parent component, in my case FoodContainer. The default value for your search state should be an empty string.

  2. Create a simple callback function to handle what's typed in the search bar. As we initially did with our filter helper function, set your search helper function to console log the text in the search bar. We want to make sure it works.

  3. Next, your Search component should take two props, the search state variable, and the helper function. We're passing state down as props because we're constructing a controlled component.

  4. In your Search component, the value should take the search state variable as props, and your onChange takes the helper callback as props. Your helper is capturing the event.target.value.

  5. Try this out to make sure it works! With the temporary console log in our search helper function in our parent component, type something into your search bar. In the console, you'll see each keystroke logged as a single letter at a time. That's ok! We're not updating state just yet; we're only console logging to ensure the callback is working correctly.

  6. Next, remove that console log and replace it with the search state's setter function. Then add one more temporary console log to check that your state is updating correctly and type again in the search bar. You'll see the text in the console and the word forming in the search bar. Cool! Because this is a controlled component with state assigned as the search value, the letters appear in the search bar as you type, aka the user is watching state update in real-time.

  7. Finally, remove that last console log.

Chaining Filter and Search together ⛓

The last step is updating the items displayed on the page based on what's being searched and filtered. In my case, I want my users to search for a specific food in the database based on the food's common name, filter by food group, and do these actions together.

My parent component FoodContainer handles displaying my food on the page, so that's where I added the following code:

  1. Declare the variable that holds the array you'll map over. I originally mapped my entire foods array, but that array didn't have my search and filter logic built in. So, I created a variable that handled my search and filter; this array will be mapped to display my items instead.

  2. Write an if statement. If the selected filter is "All," then all of the elements in the array will return. This is written as return true. Otherwise, return the elements that match the user's selected filter.

We could stop here; however, we want search and filter to work together so the user can utilize both. A couple more steps:

  1. Next, chain on another filter. Now our variable, foodsToDisplay, takes both the filter and the search code into consideration instead of one or the other. Chaining methods is essentially calling methods one after another in one statement, allowing your code to be concise and the logic to continue.

  2. In my case, I'm allowing users to search for foods by common name. includes is added because I want to return partial matches as the user types. Finally, I'm converting the common name and the search to lowercase so capitalization doesn't get in the way.

TLDR 😎 - either the user searches through all the foods or searches a filtered selection.

Now, finally, let's map over the new array! Once done, try out search and filtering in your app. It's pretty cool.
